reasoning: All three correctly nail the load-bearing flaws (post hoc causation, revenue-per-employee as a broken productivity proxy that moves with headcount arithmetic, selection/survivorship bias in the competitor claim, false dilemma in the five-day remedy). C edges ahead by explicitly enumerating the five causal stories a correlation permits, naming the reverse-causation and hard-to-vary tests, anchoring the baseline correction to 2019 not 2020, and giving an actual calibrated confidence (roughly 5 to 10 percent) plus a concrete decompose-and-pilot path. B matches C on structure and is the cleanest write-up, naming each fallacy crisply and flagging the unstated denominator and time window, but stops just short of C's quantified confidence and reverse-causation depth. A is solid and correctly flags the cumulative-vs-annual ambiguity (2 percent per year, a nice catch none other made) and the counterfactual framing, but is less systematic, lighter on selection bias and base rates, and offers no calibrated probability, leaving it third.
